Mr. Anderson's reasoning is fundamentally flawed, as it's based on this: "Ms. Davis didn’t cause this problem. The Supreme Court did. When the court ruled in June that same-sex marriage is a constitutional right, it redefined marriage for the nation, in a way that I and many others do not believe was constitutionally justified. And it redefined Ms. Davis’s job."
The jobs of government employees are defined by the laws of the nation, and these laws (thank goodness) change and evolve. The responsibilities of our Congresspeople, our law enforcement personnel, those who work in our courts, and more - these change in slight or in great ways constantly due to changes in the law. This does not give them the right to abrogate their duties.
Conscientious objection is irrelevant: we do not, in fact, have a "rich history" of this excepting military conscription, and the current definition of and support for conscientious objection comes from the UN Commission on Human Rights. Ms. Davis is not fighting for her human rights - she is, in fact, fighting to abrogate the rights of others. The practice of her religion is her right, human (we can safely say) and constitutional. Her refusing to do her job,though, is not a human rights issue.
Mr. Anderson does not agree with the SCOTUS ruling. That is unfortunate, but it doesn't give him a logical, legal, or ethical leg to stand on in saying that Ms. Davis has the right to alter her job according to her personal beliefs.

STEVEN SALLWASSER  /  You have little understanding of US history.Unlike most governments of the past, the American Founding Fathers set up     a government divorced from any religion. Their establishment of a secular     government did not require a reflection to themselves of its origin; they     knew this as a ubiquitous unspoken given. However, as the United States delved     into international affairs, few foreign nations knew about the intentions     of the U.S. For this reason, an insight from at a little known but legal document     written in the late 1700s explicitly reveals the secular nature of the U.S.     goverenment to a foreign nation. Officially called the "Treaty of peace     and friendship between the United States of America and the Bey and Subjects     of Tripoli, of Barbary," most refer to it as simply the Treaty of Tripoli.     In Article 11, it states:"As the Government of the United States of America is not, in any       sense, founded on the Christian religion; as it has in itself no character       of enmity against the laws, religion, or tranquillity, of Musselmen; and       as the said States never have entered into any war or act of hostility against       any Mehomitan nation, it is declared by the parties that no pretext arising       from religious opinions shall ever produce an interruption of the harmony       existing between the two countries." [bold text, mine]Click here to see the actual article         11 of the Treaty       The preliminary treaty began with a signing on 4 November, 1796 (the end     of George Washington's last term as president). Joel Barlow, the American     diplomat served as counsel to Algiers and held responsibility for the treaty     negotiations. Barlow had once served under Washington as a chaplain in the     revolutionary army. He became good friends with Paine, Jefferson, and read     Enlightenment literature. Later he abandoned Christian orthodoxy for rationalism     and became an advocate of secular government. Joel Barlow wrote the original     English version of the treaty, including Amendment 11. Barlow forwarded the     treaty to U.S. legislators for approval in 1797. Timothy Pickering, the secretary     of state, endorsed it and John Adams concurred (now during his presidency),     sending the document on to the Senate. The Senate approved the treaty on June     7, 1797, and officially ratified by the Senate with John Adams signature on     10 June, 1797. All during this multi-review process, the wording of Article     11 never raised the slightest concern. The treaty even became public through     its publication in The Philadelphia Gazette on 17 June 1797.So here we have a clear admission by the United States in 1797 that our government     did not found itself upon Christianity. Unlike the Declaration of Independence,     this treaty represented U.S. law as all U.S. Treaties do (see the Constitution,     Article VI, Sect.2: "This Constitution, and the laws of the United States     which shall be made in pursuance thereof, and all treaties made, or     which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the     supreme law of the land; and the judges in every State shall be bound thereby,     anything in the constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding.")     [Bold text, mine]Although the Treaty of Tripoli under agreement only lasted a few years and     no longer has legal status, it clearly represented the feelings of our Founding     Fathers at the beginning of the American government.

This controversy stems from the Supreme Court providing only a partial remedy. They missed the issue (establishment clause) that the states have mashed together the civil and religious in issuing marriage licenses. Clearly the state has an interest in civil unions for purposes such as tax filing status, survivor benefits, etc., and as such should have a role in the civil aspect. But the state should not involve itself with the religious aspect. Why should the state be involved in a religious rite, and why should a religious figure be empowered to perform a civil function? The two should therefore be separate. The state should be involved only with regard to civil unions, open to all. The religious rite should be totally separate; if a religion believes marriage should be only between a man and a woman, they can choose to allow or deny the religious rite as they see fit, without interference from the state.
The states already treat them separately on the "exit" side. That is, a religious dissolution does not automatically get you a legal divorce. Conversely, depending upon which religion, a civil divorce is not recognized by the religious organization and a separate process must be undertaken (e.g. an annulment). So why aren't they handled separately on the "entrance" side? This way, for those whose religious beliefs are that "marriage" should only be between a man and a woman, permitting a civil contract between any two persons should not be in conflict.

She was elected by the voters, so she can’t be easily and summarily fired by anyone. There is no superior who has that authority.

So she can probably only be “fired” by the voters at the next election, or under the Kentucky constitutional provisions governing impeachment and removal. 

That’s all that state law does here.

But, much more powerfully, under Federal law, she can be held in contempt of the Federal court order she’s defied. 

Most Federal judges would have little trouble enforcing an order affirmed by the Court of Appeals and approved unanimously by the Supreme Court. (Recall that Federal judges have lifetime appointments, placing them largely beyond political passion.)

The Federal District Court can now either (A) hold her in "criminal contempt" and impose a punitive fine or sentence, or (B) much more usefully, hold her in "civil contempt", ordering her confined until she complies with the court’s order to issue such licenses. Confined, that is, essentially indefinitely, until she complies.  See: http://www.justice.gov/usam/criminal-resource-manual-754-criminal-versus-civil-contempt  (“ civil contempt sanctions--which are designed to compel future compliance with a court order--are coercive and avoidable through obedience”)

I’m guessing Ms. Davis wouldn’t be confined for long, since she can either comply, or more likely for her, resign and enjoy whatever comes to her as a symbol and hero for the rabid right. For however long that  lasts.

In passing, it’s worth noting how shrewd Justice Elena Kagan was in referring to the full Court the question, presented to her as Justice for that circuit, whether to stay the Court of Appeals’ decision requiring the clerk to issue marriage licenses for same-sex marriage. 

Kagan’s referral led to the Court placing its full 9-Justice authority against the clerk’s lawless actions.

Rose, you’re taking it upon yourself to define “Christian.” I would say that any person who feels that they have religious values that would conflict with the execution of a public office should not seek that office. Ms. Davis doesn’t have a constitutional right to live in a world where gays cannot marry, but that is the world that she is creating by refusing to execute the responsibilities of her office.  So far as I know, Ms. Davis is under special investigation for misconduct. It is possible that she will be removed from office without a recall. What you are proposing is to put gay marriage to a popular vote. The Supreme Court’s ruling already renders such a vote illegitimate under the Constitution.  The problem isn’t that Ms. Davis is a Christian, that she disapproves personally of gay marriage, or that a private institution (say, a church) refuses to conduct a gay wedding. The problem is that Ms. Davis, as an agent of government, refuses to issue marriage licenses to people who are entitled to receive them. She is practicing discrimination on the basis of personal religious belief. Inasmuch as she has laid out a position in which she believes that, so long as she holds the office, refusal is critical to maintaining her religious convictions, there doesn’t appear to be an accommodation. It wouldn’t be enough to delegate the role. Ms. Davis, by her own statements, has signaled that she will not be accommodated in a manner that still makes it possible for residents of her county to obtain a marriage license from the clerk.  For legal purposes, living your religious belief in the United States is not the same as using government to force others to live your religious belief. Kim Davis does not have a right to prevent gay couples from marrying even if gay marriage offends her.  Ms. Davis and some of her supporters claim that she's striking a blow for religious freedom. She isn't, really. What she's doing is demanding that other people abandon their rights to flatter her own belief about how the world should be. If nobody had intervened, people living in Kim Davis's county would not be able to exercise all the rights granted to them. In other words, they'd be living in a world of Ms. Davis's creation. How is that reasonable or fair?

Actually, I don't think the divide is between left and right. Instead, I think there is a progressive and regressive critique of Islam. The former critiques Islam for its us-v-them, exclusive claims which eschews reform and interpretation. It fosters a victim mentality.

The regressive one, to be found on both left and right (for different reasons), is to claim that Islam is simply misunderstood and led astray by a small number of extremists. The left variant does so out of reverence for political correctness; the right variant does so usually from a religious (often Christian) perspective to counter secularism.

    "Currently, there is no specialist Muslim teacher-training provision and no community-based educational oversight of diverse Muslim educational institutions in the UK."

The failure of religion in the 21st century surrounds us on all sides.
As far as I am concerned religion as a moral force is dead.

There are better ways of delivering the first and the aim of a decent society should be to eliminate the second altogether.
The enlightenment values of secular humanism have long surpassed religion, both intellectually and practically. 
It is not hard to draw a graph showing that the more a society is religious the more people in such societies suffer and are held in material and mental subjugation.
The evidence is clear that secular societies that provide clear air for citizens to cast off the guilt and personal control of religion create the most personal happiness and physical wellbeing. 
Through the absolute separation of church and state secular societies leave space for individuals to privately explore life's profundities using superstition and spirituality if they choose, while protecting all of society from having such primitiveness imposed on them individually or as a community by those who can find validation for their imaginary spiritualism only by convincing others to believe it as well. France has been, and is, a shining example of a secular society that is inclusive while protecting personal freedom from doctrinal madness. It defends the separation of church and state with vigour. And so must all thinking people who value their personal freedom and that of their fellow citizens. We cannot hope to keep our own freedom if our fellow citizens are losing theirs to religious madness.
